questions.prompt: The two ideal op-amp assumptions for analyzing circuits with negative feedback are:
choices: A. V+ = V− and I_in = 0 (virtual short + no input current)
C. Infinite output impedance and zero gain
D. V+ ≠ V− and infinite input impedance
questions.explanation: Ideal op-amp with negative feedback: (1) virtual short: V+ = V−; (2) no input bias current: I+ = I− = 0.

questions.prompt: The inverting amplifier gain is:
questions.explanation: Inverting: A_v = −R_f / R_1. Signal is inverted (180° phase shift).

questions.prompt: The non-inverting amplifier gain is:
questions.explanation: Non-inverting: A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1. Minimum gain is 1 (voltage follower). No phase inversion.

questions.prompt: An op-amp voltage follower (buffer) has gain:
questions.explanation: Voltage follower: output connected directly to inverting input; A_v = +1. Provides high input Z, low output Z impedance matching.

questions.prompt: The output of an op-amp summing (inverting) amplifier with inputs V₁, V₂ and equal resistors R is:
questions.explanation: Inverting summing: V_out = −(R_f/R)(V₁ + V₂ + …). If R_f = R then V_out = −(V₁+V₂).

questions.prompt: The output of a difference amplifier (with equal resistors R) is:
questions.explanation: Difference amplifier: V_out = (R_f/R)(V+ − V−). Rejects common-mode signals.

questions.prompt: An op-amp integrator produces V_out = ?
questions.explanation: Inverting integrator: V_out = −1/(R_1 C_f) ∫V_in dt. Capacitor in feedback, resistor at input.

questions.prompt: An op-amp differentiator produces V_out = ?
questions.explanation: Inverting differentiator: V_out = −R_f C_1 (dV_in/dt). Capacitor at input, resistor in feedback.

questions.prompt: The slew rate of an op-amp limits:
choices: B. The maximum rate of change of output voltage (dV_out/dt)
questions.explanation: Slew rate SR = max |dV_out/dt| in V/μs. If a large signal exceeds SR, the output cannot follow — distortion occurs.

questions.prompt: The gain-bandwidth product (GBW) of an op-amp means:
questions.explanation: GBW = A_v × f_3dB = constant (for single-pole op-amps). Higher closed-loop gain → lower bandwidth.

questions.prompt: Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) of an op-amp measures:
choices: A. The ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain
questions.explanation: CMRR = A_d / A_cm (or in dB: 20 log(A_d/A_cm)). High CMRR → rejects noise common to both inputs.

questions.prompt: An op-amp comparator without feedback:
choices: A. Operates in the linear region
B. Saturates at V+ or V− supply when V_in exceeds reference
D. Must use the virtual short assumption
questions.explanation: Comparator (no negative feedback): output slams to +V_sat or −V_sat. Cannot apply virtual short assumption.

questions.prompt: A Schmitt trigger (comparator with positive feedback) provides:
choices: A. Hysteresis — two different threshold voltages for rising and falling inputs
C. Negative feedback to stabilize gain
questions.explanation: Schmitt trigger: positive feedback creates hysteresis between V_TL (lower) and V_TH (upper) thresholds. Prevents noisy switching.

questions.prompt: An inverting amplifier has R_1 = 10 kΩ and R_f = 100 kΩ. What is the gain?
The input offset voltage (V_OS) of an op-amp is:
choices: A. The differential voltage at the inputs when the output is zero
D. Zero for all real op-amps
questions.explanation: V_OS: small differential input voltage (mV) required to make V_out = 0. Causes DC errors in precision circuits.

questions.prompt: Negative feedback in op-amp circuits:
choices: A. Increases distortion and decreases bandwidth
B. Reduces gain but improves bandwidth, reduces distortion, stabilizes gain
D. Is only useful in comparators
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: trading gain for bandwidth, linearity, stability, and predictable closed-loop behavior.

questions.prompt: An instrumentation amplifier offers _____ over a simple difference amplifier.
choices: B. Very high input impedance and high CMRR
questions.explanation: Instrumentation amp (3 op-amps): high Z differential inputs, high CMRR, programmable gain via single resistor.

questions.prompt: Which applications commonly use op-amps? (Select ALL that apply.)
questions.explanation: Op-amps are used in active filters, DAC/ADC circuits, amplifiers, integrators, comparators, etc. Not for digital logic gates (those use logic IC families).

questions.prompt: A non-inverting amplifier has R_1 = 1 kΩ and R_f = 9 kΩ. What is the closed-loop gain?
Op-amp output saturation occurs when:
choices: A. The calculated linear output exceeds the supply rails (±V_sat)
B. The input current exceeds I_bias
D. The slew rate is less than 1 V/μs
questions.explanation: Saturation: when the ideal V_out would exceed ±(V_CC − ~1 V), the output clips at ±V_sat.

questions.prompt: An op-amp has GBW = 1 MHz and is configured for A_v = 20 V/V. What is its −3 dB bandwidth in kHz?
questions.explanation: f_3dB = GBW / A_v = 1 MHz / 20 = 50 kHz

questions.prompt: For maximum full-power bandwidth (FPBW) of a sinusoidal output V_pk at frequency f, the slew rate must satisfy:
questions.explanation: FPBW: SR = 2πf V_pk → f_FPBW = SR / (2π V_pk). The derivative of V_pk sin(2πft) peaks at 2πf V_pk.

questions.prompt: A transimpedance amplifier (current-to-voltage converter) uses an op-amp with:
choices: A. Resistor R_f in feedback; input current I_in connects to inverting input; V_out = −I_in R_f
D. R_f at the non-inverting input
questions.explanation: Transimpedance: I_in → inverting input (virtual ground), R_f in feedback; V_out = −I_in × R_f. Used with photodiodes.

questions.prompt: The power supply rejection ratio (PSRR) of an op-amp measures:
choices: A. The op-amp's ability to reject supply voltage variations from appearing at the output
B. The ratio of output power to input power
questions.explanation: PSRR = ΔV_supply / ΔV_output (or the equivalent input offset voltage change). High PSRR → noise on supply does not affect output.

questions.prompt: An op-amp active low-pass filter using an inverting topology with C_f in parallel with R_f has −3 dB corner frequency:
questions.explanation: Inverting low-pass: Z_f = R_f ‖ (1/jωC_f); corner when R_f = 1/(ωC_f) → f_c = 1/(2πR_f C_f).
